title: Bonds, yields, and the price-rate relationship
subtitle: The inverse relationship between bond prices and yields is one of the most important mechanics in finance — and one of the least intuitively understood.

body.content: Here's the thing about bonds that trips everyone up: when interest rates go up, bond prices go down. And vice versa. It feels counterintuitive — why would a 'safe' asset lose value? But it's not mysterious at all once you see the math. Let's walk through it.

You buy a bond for $1,000. It pays 3% interest — so $30 per year. Now, one year later, new bonds are being issued at 5%. Your bond still pays $30 per year. Anyone buying in the market today can get $50 per year for $1,000. Why would they pay you $1,000 for a bond that pays $30? They wouldn't. They'd only buy your bond at a discount — at a price low enough that the $30 annual payment represents a 5% return on what they paid. That price is $600. Your bond just 'lost' 40% of its value, even though nothing about the bond itself changed.

body.label: The math
body.content: $1,000 bond, 3% coupon = $30/year. If prevailing rates rise to 5%, price must fall to ~$600 for the yield to match. $30 / $600 = 5%. The bond's cash flows haven't changed. The price adjusted so the yield matches the market. This is the entire inverse relationship, and it's just arithmetic.
Duration: how sensitive is your bond?

Not all bonds react the same way to rate changes. Duration measures this sensitivity. A bond with duration of 8 years will lose approximately 8% in price for every 1% rise in rates. A bond with duration of 2 years loses only 2%. Short-duration bonds are far more stable when rates move.

items: 2-year Treasury note: duration ~1.9 years — minimal rate sensitivity
10-year Treasury note: duration ~8-9 years — moderate sensitivity
30-year Treasury bond: duration ~18-20 years — high sensitivity
A 1% rate rise on a 20-year duration bond = roughly 20% price drop

body.content: This is why 2022 was a brutal year for bond investors. The Fed raised rates by 4.25 percentage points in 9 months. Holders of long-duration bond funds saw losses of 25-40%. These were instruments people held for safety. Understanding duration would have told you exactly how much risk you were taking.
Yield to maturity vs. coupon rate

The coupon rate is fixed — it's what the bond pays based on its face value. The yield to maturity (YTM) is the actual return you'll earn if you buy the bond at the current market price and hold it to maturity. These are often different. A bond trading below face value has a YTM higher than its coupon. A bond trading above face value has a YTM lower than its coupon. Always look at YTM when comparing bonds — not the coupon.
Credit ratings and the risk premium

Not all bonds are equal. US Treasury bonds have essentially zero credit risk — the US government can print dollars. Corporate bonds carry the risk that the company might default. The extra yield a corporate bond pays above a comparable Treasury is called the credit spread. Investment grade spreads are tight. High-yield ('junk') spreads are wide. The spread is the market's real-time pricing of credit risk.

body.label: Watch out
body.content: High yield means high risk — not high safety. A bond yielding 10% when Treasuries yield 5% is offering a 5% credit spread. The market is telling you there's a meaningful chance this borrower doesn't pay you back. Chasing yield without understanding why it's high is one of the oldest mistakes in fixed income investing.

Bond prices fall when rates rise — that's arithmetic, not opinion. Duration tells you how sensitive your bond is. YTM is the number that actually matters when comparing bonds. And high yield always means higher credit risk. Understand these mechanics and bonds stop being mysterious.
